questionText: Simplify $\dfrac{\sqrt{8} + \sqrt{18}}{\sqrt{2}}$, giving your answer as an integer.

steps.description: Simplify the first surd on top.
steps.explanation: Taking out the square factor $4$ turns $\sqrt8$ into $2\sqrt2$, so the numerator becomes a multiple of $\sqrt2$.

steps.description: Simplify the second surd on top.
steps.explanation: Taking out the square factor $9$ turns $\sqrt{18}$ into $3\sqrt2$.

steps.description: Add the like surds in the numerator.
steps.explanation: Both terms are multiples of $\sqrt2$, so we add the coefficients to get $5\sqrt2$.
Replacing the numerator with its simplified form gives a much cleaner fraction.
The $\sqrt2$ on top and bottom are identical, so they divide to $1$.
Any non-zero quantity divided by itself is $1$, clearing the surd entirely.

steps.description: Confirm the answer is an integer.
steps.explanation: The surds have cancelled completely, leaving the whole number $5$, as required.
Combining the roots first gives $5\sqrt{1}=5$, confirming the result by a second route.
Approximate decimals give about $5$, a useful sanity check on the exact answer.
Simplifying each surd first turned an awkward-looking fraction into a simple cancellation — the key strategy here.

steps.workingLatex: \text{numerator was a multiple of the denominator}
steps.explanation: Because the numerator reduced to $5\sqrt2$ and the denominator was $\sqrt2$, the division was exact.
The simplified value of the expression is the integer $5$.
